reasoning: The Fed has currently held rates steady at 3.50% - 3.75%, with the EFFR at 3.64% for the last four months. The March 2026 FOMC dot plot revealed expectations for zero or 1 rate cut by the end of 2026, with NO participants projecting an increase. Thus, the near-term probabilities (2026) are assigned the minimum or near-minimum probability of 0.05-0.10, scaling slightly to reflect tiny unforeseeable changes or EFFR jitter. As the time horizon increases to 2027 and 2029, the uncertainty of a new hiking cycle starts to balance the initial likely cut cycle, pulling probabilities upward. Chairman Powell indicated that the current 3.5-3.75% range represents a "neutral stance." Assuming long-term neutrality is around 3.6%, the fed funds rate has roughly equal chances to be above or below 3.64% in the distant long run. Therefore, out-year predictions (2031, 2036) approach 0.50, successfully reflecting maximum horizon uncertainty.
